claims.claim_text: The CKA treats troubleshooting as a large exam domain, and the exam is a time-pressured performance-based test.
claims.rationale: The Linux Foundation CKA page directly lists the Troubleshooting domain and exam duration, but the host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Core control-plane components have distinct responsibilities: kube-apiserver exposes the Kubernetes HTTP API, etcd stores API data, kube-scheduler assigns unscheduled Pods to Nodes, and kube-controller-manager runs controllers that implement Kubernetes API behavior.
claims.span_hint: section: 2.1 Control Plane Components and Their Failure Shapes
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes components overview states these responsibilities directly.

claims.claim_text: Pods begin in Pending, move to Running when at least one primary container starts, and the kubelet manages container lifecycle and probes as part of Pod lifecycle handling.
claims.span_hint: section: 1.4 Inspect Evidence in an Order That Matches the Lifecycle
claims.rationale: The Pod lifecycle page explicitly defines the phase progression and notes that the kubelet manages containers and probes.

claims.claim_text: `kubectl describe pod` exposes container state, readiness, restart count, and recent Events, and the Ready condition indicates a Pod should be added to the load-balancing pools of matching Services.
claims.rationale: The debug-running-pod guide describes these `describe` fields and states what the Ready condition means.

claims.claim_text: `kubectl logs --previous` retrieves logs from the previous instance of a container in a Pod.
claims.rationale: The autogenerated kubectl reference defines `--previous` exactly this way.

claims.claim_text: `CrashLoopBackOff` can appear in kubectl Status output for a repeatedly failing Pod, but it is not the Pod phase.
claims.span_hint: section: 4 Reading Pod Status Without Being Fooled
claims.rationale: The Pod lifecycle page explicitly warns not to confuse kubectl Status values like `CrashLoopBackOff` with Pod phase.

claims.claim_text: After DNS resolution succeeds, Service debugging still requires checking EndpointSlices and port mapping; empty endpoints usually indicate selector and label mismatch, and `targetPort` or named ports must match Pod ports.
claims.rationale: The official service debugging guide walks through DNS success, EndpointSlice verification, selector mismatches, and targetPort checks.

claims.claim_text: The Kubernetes API server exposes `healthz`, `livez`, and `readyz` endpoints, and `healthz` is deprecated in favor of the more specific readiness and liveness endpoints.
claims.span_hint: section: 1.1 Observe the Symptom Without Mutating the Cluster
claims.rationale: The API health-check reference states the available endpoints and the deprecation status of `healthz`.

claims.claim_text: On kubeadm-built clusters, control-plane components are static Pods in `kube-system` sourced from `/etc/kubernetes/manifests`.
claims.span_hint: section: 3.7 Node and Control Plane Checks
claims.rationale: The kubeadm implementation details page explicitly says kubeadm writes static Pod manifests for control-plane components to `/etc/kubernetes/manifests` and deploys them in `kube-system`.

claims.claim_text: NetworkPolicies select Pods and define allowed ingress or egress with selectors; when a Pod is isolated, only traffic allowed by applicable policies is permitted.
claims.rationale: The NetworkPolicy concept page directly defines selector-based policy application and ingress/egress isolation behavior.

claims.claim_text: PersistentVolumeClaims remain unbound until a matching volume exists, and once a claim is bound Kubernetes mounts the bound volume into Pods that use that claim.
claims.rationale: The Persistent Volumes concept page explicitly describes unbound claims and the mount behavior once a claim is bound and used by a Pod.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Events are namespaced high-level operational signals, and `kubectl describe pod` plus `kubectl get events` are standard ways to inspect recent scheduling and runtime events.
claims.rationale: The debug-running-pod guide explains that events shown by `describe` are recent component-generated signals and notes that events are namespaced and listable with `kubectl get events`.

further_reading.title: Debug Running Pods
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary reference for `describe`, logs, Events, readiness, and node-vs-pod troubleshooting order.

further_reading.title: Debug Services
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the exact service triage flow the module teaches: DNS, selectors, EndpointSlices, and port mapping.

further_reading.title: Pod Lifecycle
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for Pod phases, kubelet lifecycle handling, readiness context, and `CrashLoopBackOff` terminology.

notes: Section pool reference left null because no shared pool file was provided. Quiz items, exercise steps, code blocks, diagrams, and table-only rows were intentionally excluded unless the surrounding prose made a distinct cite-worthy claim.
